metadata.title: What Religion Affords Grassroots NGOs: Frames, Networks, Modes of Action
metadata.journal: Journal for the Scientific Study of Religion

abstract: The international relief and development sector has swelled in the last two decades thanks to American grassroots NGOs: groups that rely on volunteer labor and individual contributions, often on budgets of less than $25,000 a year. Most of these organizations reject the label of "faith-based organization," yet they find the symbolic and material resources of religion indispensable. Religion affords these NGOs three kinds of resources to meet their distinct organizational needs. First, it provides frames, or ways of thinking and speaking about relief and development work that imbue it with legitimacy. Next, religion offers networks that provide money, volunteers, and entrée into aid-receiving communities. Finally, religion affords familiar modes of action that link the NGO, supporters, and local aid recipients. I support these claims with LDA topic modeling (a computerized method of text analysis), content analysis of websites, and in-depth interviews with 43 informants.

paragraphs: Natalie, the 30-year-old founder of For Kenya's Tomorrow (FKT), illustrates the difficulty in designating an international aid group as religious. Both Natalie and her Kenyan husband are Pentecostals; her mother-in-law is the pastor of a small Nairobi church that is the key partner for aid activities, and the bulk of FKT's funding and volunteers come from Natalie's childhood church in Michigan. A recent newsletter punctuates the group's successes with the exclamation "PRAISE JESUS!" And yet Natalie does not describe For Kenya's Tomorrow as a "faith-based" or "religious" organization. For her, to be a "religious organization" is to restrict volunteering to those who share the same faith, or to offer its services only to fellow believers. It also suggests that an organization is claiming a divine mandate for its work-"doing things in my name." She fears that Christian groups abuse this mantle, "doing things . . . that aren't a true representation of who I [God] am." To be more authentically Christian, then, is to eschew the label of "religious" but to "lead by example." For Kenya's Tomorrow defines itself as a secular organization even though religion shapes its programs, its partners, and its leaders' decisions.

Consider also Erasto, a Tanzanian immigrant in his 40s working in Minnesota as a computer engineer. A decade ago, Erasto and his American-born wife decided to start a secondary school in his native village in Tanzania. Friends suggested that Erasto meet with a local Lutheran minister who had traveled several times to Tanzania through a sister-church program. They soon discovered that the pastor had not only visited Erasto's village, but had met his mother. Pastor Dan's church became a dedicated donor to the school, and until the school was legally incorporated as an independent nonprofit its funds were wired through the Saint Paul Area Synod. Why would an immigrant with wholly secular development objectives team up with a Lutheran pastor? And why would an aid group infused with Pentecostalism reject the label of "faith-based"? This article shows that this kind of relationship to religion-where a group does not formally define itself as "religious," but draws on the symbolic and material resources of religion-is common in a large population of small, U.S.-based international aid groups. These organizations have particular needs that arise from their size and institutional context, and I show that religion helps meet these needs, even as it imposes constraints.

Natalie's and Erasto's organizations are two of more than 10,000 international relief and development NGOs (nongovernmental organizations) started by Americans since 1990. This nonprofit subsector includes well-known groups like CARE and World Vision, but its remarkable growth in the last two and half decades has been driven by groups that operate on volunteer labor and budgets of less than $25,000 per year (Schnable 2015). These groups are headquartered in every U.S. state and are typically started by an individual who has a tie to the global South through immigration, work, or previous tourism. Most of the groups' founders hold a college degree but have no training or professional experience in international development. To distinguish these groups from counterparts like CARE that employ large professional staffs and operate on sizable budgets-often with substantial grant or government contract income-I refer to them as American grassroots NGOs. 2In the same time period as the growth of grassroots NGOs has been a dramatic increase in policy and academic attention to the place of religion in international development. The World Bank under James Wolfensohn initiated the World Faiths Development Dialogue, which later found an academic home at the Berkley Center for Religion, Peace and World Affairs at Georgetown University. Subsequently, the Swiss, Swedish, and British foreign aid agencies launched various initiatives related to the role of religion in development. (The American foreign aid agency, USAID, was meanwhile included in the Bush administration's broader effort to engage faith-based organizations with federal agencies.) The dearth of articles related to religion in academic development journals-ver Beek (2000) In the early years of these discussions, writers often made stark distinctions between "faithbased" and "secular" groups (Jones and Petersen 2011), but that dichotomy is eroding as scholars explore the variety of organizations involved in development and identify the many ways religion operates within them (Clarke and Jennings 2008; James 2009; Swidler 2013; Thaut 2009). 3 McCleary (2009) has shown that the apparently clear religious identities of denominational aid agencies became compartmentalized as some groups partnered with the U.S. government starting in the 1970s, or were abandoned altogether. A study by Kniss and Campbell (1997) of 63 American religious aid groups showed that religious identities had little effect on organizations' program size or finances. And other work has shown that religious aid organizations come to look like their secular peers as regulations from funders, the sensibilities of host governments, and the normative pressures of professional aid work exact isomorphism (Agensky 2013;Bornstein 2001;Burchardt 2013).

What all of this suggests is that the institutional context matters a great deal for how religion operates in aid organizations. This article shows how religion functions in a large population of small American aid organizations that operate in a very different institutional context than those featured in the literature to date. Grassroots NGOs are not congregations, missions, or denominational aid agencies. They are not large secular aid agencies that are learning to make sense of religion as a kind of second language (e.g., Marshall 2011;van der Wel 2011), nor are they large faith-based agencies like World Vision that have to bow to the norms of the professional aid field (Agensky 2013;Bornstein 2001). American grassroots NGOs have distinctive organizational needs compared with those other groups. This article explains how grassroots NGOs are different from the aid groups that social scientists have studied before, and it identifies three religious resources commonly used by these groups. I also show ways that religious resources can be inadequate or constraining, prodding the leaders of grassroots NGOs to employ alternatives.

In the next section, I describe American grassroots NGOs and explain how their organizational qualities shape their relationship to religion. This analysis is part of a larger research project on grassroots NGOs, and in the "Data and Methods" section of the article, I describe the LDA topic modeling, content analysis of websites, and case study interviews used to analyze these groups. The bulk of the article illustrates how grassroots NGOs use three categories of religious resources. First are frames, or ways of thinking and speaking about relief and development work that imbue it with legitimacy. Next are networks of congregations and individuals that provide goods, personnel, and entrée into aid-receiving communities. Third are modes of action that link the NGO, supporters, and local aid recipients.

sections.paper_id: Schnable - 2016 - What Religion Affords Grassroots NGOs Frames, Networks, Modes of Action
sections.header_raw: AMERICAN GRASSROOTS NGOS AND THEIR INSTITUTIONAL CONTEXT
sections.header_norm: american grassroots ngos and their institutional context
paragraphs: The "development project" has been one of the defining tasks of the post-World War II world (Ferguson 2000). Private aid agencies-including several large denominational groupsplayed important roles in recovery from World War II (Smith 1990), and the role for NGOs in development began to expand further in the 1970s (Babb 2009). Technological shifts in the last two decades have lowered the barriers to entry for new aid organizations. Broad cell phone coverage in the global South, free e-mail, and cheap container shipping and international flights have made it possible to operate part-time projects on a small budget. Would-be aid workers no longer need to join a firm-they can launch a start-up. Rather than being stamped with the imprimatur of a sending organization (government/voluntary, religious/secular), grassroots aid groups like For Kenya's Tomorrow reflect the multivalent interests and orientations of their founders.

These organizations operate a mixture of relief and development projects. Some groups offer humanitarian relief in the wake of natural disasters. (A wave of new organizations was formed immediately after the 2010 Haiti earthquake.) Others provide clean water or training in agriculture techniques. Programs to bring American medical personnel to underserved areas are common, as are projects focused on education, microenterprise, and care for orphans. A smaller segment of groups proselytizes and trains clergy, but grassroots NGOs most often pair these efforts with social welfare projects.

Most grassroots NGOs lack full-time staff in the United States, and their founders (who do the lion's share of the work) remain embedded in their careers and personal networks in the United States, unlike full-time, trained aid workers whose orientation is to the professional field of development (Mosse 2011). Most grassroots NGOs are not affiliated with NGO intermediary organizations like Interaction or the Association of Evangelical Relief and Development Organizations. Because they rely on private funds and are not integrated into the professional development field, grassroots NGOs are insulated from the normative pressures facing larger aid organizations. The entrepreneurial nature of grassroots NGOs gives founders broad latitude in shaping their programs, allowing them to draw on "cultured capacities" (Swidler 2001) learned from work, family, or religious life. But while the leaders of grassroots NGOs have relative freedom in the models they employ, they face several acute challenges compared with other aid efforts.

First, these groups' voluntary status and small size means that they lack professional legitimacy vis-à-vis potential supporters and partners. NGO leaders can try to build legitimacy with religious discourse that frames their chosen projects in religious traditions of charitable action-or that even suggests divine sanction for the activities (the discursive move that Natalie was reluctant to make). Second, these groups face problems of money and labor. All nonprofit organizations must raise funds, and Agensky (2013), Reynolds (2013), and others have written about ways that larger religious NGOs court religious donors. Grassroots NGOs are distinctive because they rely almost exclusively on individual donors within the leaders' personal networks for support. I show below ways that grassroots NGOs draw on familiar religious modes of action about charitable giving-to varying levels of success. Finally, one of grassroots NGOs' fundamental tasks is to gain a foothold in prospective field sites and to build effective relationships there. They lack connections with host country governments and the other institutions with which large aid agencies typically collaborate. Here grassroots NGOs use religious networks to gain introductions and religious modes of action shared with aid recipients to build partnerships.

I analyze the relationship between religion and grassroots organizations, with their particular needs described above, as one of resource affordance. To see religion as a set of resources rather than a fixed identity is consistent with the "tool box" perspective on culture (Swidler 1986) and more specifically a view of religious incongruence (Chaves 2010). While this perspective is widely embraced in the scientific study of religion, I emphasize it here because a religious essentialism-good people do good-has hovered in the development policy literature (Jones and Petersen 2011;Malloch 2003). We know that religious behavior is fragmented and contextual, and that religious belief or affiliation themselves do not automatically yield generosity (Norenzayan and Shariff 2008). By looking at religion as affording aid groups certain resources, we are mindful that religious identities or beliefs do not automatically generate more or better assistance to the poor.

Private aid agencies-including several large denominational groupsplayed important roles in recovery from World War II (Smith 1990), and the role for NGOs in development began to expand further in the 1970s (Babb 2009). Technological shifts in the last two decades have lowered the barriers to entry for new aid organizations.
sections.preview_tail: While this perspective is widely embraced in the scientific study of religion, I emphasize it here because a religious essentialism-good people do good-has hovered in the development policy literature (Jones and Petersen 2011;Malloch 2003). We know that religious behavior is fragmented and contextual, and that religious belief or affiliation themselves do not automatically yield generosity (Norenzayan and Shariff 2008). By looking at religion as affording aid groups certain resources, we are mindful that religious identities or beliefs do not automatically generate more or better assistance to the poor.

These groups were drawn from the universe of 501(c)3 organizations coded by the IRS as International Relief, Development, or Human Rights organizations, which totaled 10,624 active groups at the end of 2011. 4 This confines the analysis to American-based groups and excludes what Watkins, Swidler, and Hannan (2012) call "free-floating altruists," individuals doing aid work without a registered organization, as well as social enterprises that aim for some public benefit but are incorporated as businesses rather than nonprofit entities. Also excluded are groups that identify themselves as an organization with primarily religious objectives, since such groups are given a different purpose category (NTEE code) by the IRS. That all of the organizations in this sample declare their main purpose to be relief or development makes their use of religious resources all the more striking.

Creating the database of NGO websites and assembling a text corpus was one of the early tasks of the research project. The strengths of website data are their ubiquity and orientation towards the interested public, and in many cases websites constitute the main or only public source of information on NGOs' projects, volunteers or staff, and global locations. The IRS does not require organizations to provide a web address and there is no other central clearinghouse for these sites, so I created a database of NGO websites with help from Amazon Mechanical Turk 5 workers. Of 10,624 organizations registered with the IRS by the end of 2011, web addresses were found for 6,749, with all but 174 of those yielding functioning sites.

I analyzed this corpus with LDA topic modeling (Blei and Lafferty 2007), an inductive technique that uses computer algorithms to analyze the distribution of words in each website to look for patterns of co-occurrence, called topics. The LDA software uses Bayesian modeling to generate lists of words that disproportionately co-occur, and the researcher must use her knowledge of the corpus to identify the common thread. For example, DiMaggio, Nag, and Blei (2013) undertook a similar analysis of articles on the arts in five national newspapers from 1986 to 1997 and found topics about culture wars, urban space, and grants to arts organizations. Once the topics have been established, researchers can use the LDA program to identify which topics are prevalent in each document in the corpus. (See Appendix 2 for a more detailed discussion of the statistical assumptions and mechanics of LDA.) In this instance, 24 meaningful topics emerged from the corpus. Among these are topics about child sponsorship, ecology, human rights, foreign policy, war, humanitarian relief, and Christian ministry. (A list of the 10 most prevalent terms for each topic is given in Appendix 1.) The proportion of each document (website) constituted by each topic can also be determined. I define a topic as being present in a document if 5 percent of the document's text comes from that topic. In the subsequent analysis I discuss the relative prevalence of the Christian ministry topic and its relationship to other topics in the text.

While topic modeling allows broad analysis of themes across the corpus of websites, indepth content analysis of a random sample (n = 150) of these organizations' websites provides information about the projects and locations of NGOs as well as a more nuanced view of their discourse. Each website was captured as a PDF file coded with 152 codes in the categories of 4 When an organization applies for tax-exempt status with the IRS, it is given a code from the National Taxonomy of Exempt Entities (NTEE). Based on each code's detailed description in the NTEE-CC handbook and analysis of a sample of organizations with the relevant codes, I include organizations in the IRS's Business Master File with the following codes Q30-39: International Development and Relief and Q70-71: International Human Rights. Web searches of a random sample of organizations with these codes revealed that organizations labeled as "human rights" organizations and those labeled as "development" were carrying out very similar work. 5 Amazon Mechanical Turk is a clearinghouse to outsource large numbers of small tasks that require human intelligence. For a discussion of the quality of data generated with Mechanical Turk, see Kittur, Chi, and Suh (2008). location, aid context, donor, mode of action, networks, rationales, recipients, and volunteers. 6 Narrative accounts of the work and "About Us" and "Get Involved" pages yield other sorts of information-on the roles that potential supporters can play and the organizational partners already connected to the NGO. Accounts of projects and fundraising events and other pages that name "partners" reveal a host of network connections: other NGOs, businesses, religious congregations, government agencies, and civic groups. In this article I refer especially to the codes for network ties, rationales (which indicate religious framing), and network partners (in which religious congregations figure prominently). Descriptive statistics on project sector, location of aid work, recipients, rationales, and network partners is given in Table 1 .

The final, complementary source of data is interviews with 43 informants from five grassroots NGOs that were chosen for in-depth case studies. The five case study NGOs were based in East Africa, the most common destination region for grassroots NGOs. The five NGOs varied in revenue, the sector in which they worked, the target recipients, and whether they self-identified as secular or religious. Features of each organization (named with pseudonyms 7 ) are described in Table 2. For each of these organizations, 5-12 in-depth interviews were conducted with the founders, board members, key staff or volunteers, and a small number of the organizations' beneficiaries. All but two of the 43 interviews were conducted in person in the U.S. city where the NGO is based or in the organization's East Africa project location. The interviews were digitally recorded and later transcribed.

The topic modeling, content analysis, and case studies make different tradeoffs of scope and detail. Content analysis and the case studies support the discussion of frames, modes of action, and networks, while topic modeling is used only in the discussion of frames. Taken together, these three forms of data offer a triangulated view of the symbolic and material resources that religion affords grassroots NGOs.

paragraphs: Religion offers the leaders of grassroots NGOs frames: ways of organizing and rendering meaningful the problems of global poverty and the group's proposed solutions. Grassroots NGOs' projects could be (and historically have been) framed in several ways. For example, schooling and agriculture were framed as "civilizing" projects under colonial regimes, as "modernization" in the post-World War II era, and most recently as "development" or "poverty alleviation" (Ferguson 2000;Frank and Meyer 2007). Leaders of grassroots NGOs also frame these tasks as part of religious traditions of charity, altruism, or mission. By doing this, grassroots NGOs use an alternative path to legitimacy; one doesn't need an advanced degree or government mandate to intervene.

By frame, I mean "a lens through which we observe and interpret social life . . . [that can] highlight certain aspects of social life and hide or block others" (Small, Harding, and Lamont 2010:14;Goffman 1974;Snow andBenford 1988, 1992). When grassroots NGOs use religious frames, they highlight religious justifications for charity and sometimes for particular projects or classes of recipients. Other religious discourse can suggest a calling or a divine sanction to a 6 The sample can be interpreted as probabilistically representative of all organizations with websites but not of all NGOs. A comparison of IRS data for a sample of organizations with and without websites showed that NGOs with websites had higher average income and were older than NGOs without websites at statistically significant levels. 7 The chairman of Indego Africa preferred the use of the true name for the organization of previously published accounts of the group. Religious language is a common feature of NGO websites, and it is used to frame projects ranging from training clergy to providing water. In the LDA topic model, one of the 24 substantive topics that emerged was one that I labeled "Christian ministry." It had as its most common terms, in descending order: church, ministry, mission, God, Christian, prayer, Jesus, praise, missionary, pastor, Christ, faith. This topic appeared in 1,670, or 25 percent, of the websites analyzed. Some of the groups that use this language work explicitly on evangelistic tasks: proselytizing, training clergy, and so on. But in many cases the language is used to frame programs that are not inherently religious or secular. Of websites where the Christian ministry topic is present, 53 percent feature the "community development " topic, 24 percent the "humanitarian relief " topic, 44 percent the "child sponsorship" topic, and 21 percent the "microenterprise" topic.

The hand-coded sample of 150 websites confirmed that religious language was used to frame a variety of projects. Of these 150 sites, the rationales given by 41 of these groups related to enacting God's will or demonstrating God's love. Of these, 28 were carrying out the sort of Christian evangelistic tasks described above, typically in concert with social welfare projects. Of the remaining 13, two carried out social welfare and acts of Islamic charity, and 11 worked on orphanage, water, library, political advocacy, or microenterprise projects with no evangelistic component. 8A prime example of religious framing comes from the group Living Water International: topic modeling shows its website to be composed mainly of topics for water (81 percent) and Christian ministry (5 percent). On a page of the site titled "Why Water?," the organization offers a number of reasons why water and sanitation are pressing issues for development. The page cites statistics on water-borne illness from the World Health Organization and the medical journal The Lancet: water-related diseases cause 2.2 million deaths a year, and roughly 2,000 children succumb to diarrheal diseases each day in Africa. But the most prominent text on the page frames water in terms of the gospel: No a 501(c)3 organizations with less than $50,000 in revenue are not required to report an exact amount to the IRS with their annual Form 990 filing. Their revenues are recorded by the IRS as $0.
The revenue figures here for the Rwanda Ultrasound Initiative and For Kenya's Tomorrow were given by the organizations' presidents during interviews.

14685906, 2016, 2, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/jssr.12272 by University Of Chicago Library, Wiley Online Library on [02/02/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License For Living Water it's all about Jesus. It's about demonstrating God's love, announcing his kingdom, seeing Jesus in the least of our brothers and sisters, offering a cup of water in Jesus' name and proclaiming his gospel, the living water. Helping communities create sustainable water, sanitation, hygiene, and Christian witness programs in partnership with local churches is just the best way we've found to do that. Why? Because the water crisis affects poverty, women, health and education-and for us it's a spiritual issue. (Living Water International 2015)

The NGO's name and this text refer to two passages in the gospel of John where Jesus speaks of living water: "If any one thirst, let him come to me and drink. He who believes in me, as the scripture has said, 'Out of his heart shall flow rivers of living water'" (John 7:37-38, AV). The group uses claims from development experts about the effect of water shortages on women, health, education, and poverty. But using the phrase "living water" invokes the ministry of Jesus and suggests to the reader that digging wells is in continuity with that ministry. The allusion is what allows the group to say "for us it's a spiritual issue," and to enlist supporters on the basis of the project's religious bona fides.

Tying a project to a passage of scripture is one way to frame it as "religious." Other techniques are to describe the project as a vessel of God's love, or to claim that it arose from God's call to an individual to carry it out. Even projects without an explicit biblical link or in common traditions of religious charity can be framed as expressions of God's love, as seen by the discourse of an NGO doing women's enterprise projects:

OneMaker exists to be a tangible expression of God's love to poor women and girls vulnerable to trafficking and other exploitation by giving opportunities through education and business ventures. We provide educational sponsorships to girls in poverty, believing their God-given potential is a treasure and should be developed. (Onemaker 2013) In this text, "potential"-intellectual and economic-is a gift from God, and programs that support that potential are an extension of God's love.

It is not uncommon in these texts for NGO leaders-or volunteers for the organization posting blogs or comments on the website-to offer narratives of God "calling" them to start the organization or to engage in a certain project. In one example, the leader of a group that operates orphan programs in Africa blogs about a late-night prayer session where she is told to prepare a space for infants. In the account below from the Friends of UNIFAT website (2015), a Ugandan woman describes her desolation after the civil war in that country, and how a prayer "led" her to start a school that would later receive support from an American NGO:

As I prayed to be shown what to do, I was led to the parable about the sower who went sowing seeds . . . . I knew then that the good ground could represent children and the sower could be I; the seeds could be the good information, or teaching imparted to the children. I knew that the time had come. In the spring of 1983 I returned home to start a school among my people.

Describing one's efforts as led by God can do several kinds of discursive work. It might presage success, or potential for success, especially if it is linked with religious narratives with a known outcome. A reader of this text who is familiar with the parable of the seeds knows that the seeds sown on "good ground" bear fruit. If God brought this text to the woman's mind, along with the vision of a school, a reader may believe that God will bless the school's efforts with "fruits." Second, describing one's efforts as a calling can justify the choice of a particular place or type of project that might otherwise seem arbitrary. After a civil war, why start a school, rather than rebuilding houses or sheltering orphans? Claiming one is "shown what to do" by God provides purchase for a particular approach to complex problems of poverty.

Religious framing can generate the legitimacy that grassroots NGOs need, but it is not always rhetorically successful. A volunteer from Wellsprings of Hope described her efforts to find sponsors for Ugandan children with an evangelical frame of "witness." But her family members saw Wellsprings of Hope as just another charity looking for cash. She told me about her frustration with herself, believing that she hadn't done enough to gain support for the cause:
Volunteer: So none of my . . . my family doesn't sponsor. They pray for me. They know I'm active. I . . . Interviewer: Yeah. They probably have their own passions too, right?
Volunteer: I don't know. I don't know. I feel like since I've been so frustrated that I haven't been a good enough witness or something. (Emphasis mine.)

Unlike professional fundraisers, grassroots NGO volunteers rely on frames that are personally salient to them; for this volunteer, the religious frame was the only salient one. Understanding Wellsprings' work only from an evangelistic perspective constrained her from explaining it to donors in ways that were more appealing to them. It also created a sense of personal frustration because she saw herself as a failed "witness" rather a spokesperson for one among many worthy aid efforts.

Other techniques are to describe the project as a vessel of God's love, or to claim that it arose from God's call to an individual to carry it out. Even projects without an explicit biblical link or in common traditions of religious charity can be framed as expressions of God's love, as seen by the discourse of an NGO doing women's enterprise projects:

OneMaker exists to be a tangible expression of God's love to poor women and girls vulnerable to trafficking and other exploitation by giving opportunities through education and business ventures. We provide educational sponsorships to girls in poverty, believing their God-given potential is a treasure and should be developed. (Onemaker 2013) In this text, "potential"-intellectual and economic-is a gift from God, and programs that support that potential are an extension of God's love.

It is not uncommon in these texts for NGO leaders-or volunteers for the organization posting blogs or comments on the website-to offer narratives of God "calling" them to start the organization or to engage in a certain project. In one example, the leader of a group that operates orphan programs in Africa blogs about a late-night prayer session where she is told to prepare a space for infants. In the account below from the Friends of UNIFAT website (2015), a Ugandan woman describes her desolation after the civil war in that country, and how a prayer "led" her to start a school that would later receive support from an American NGO:

I knew then that the good ground could represent children and the sower could be I; the seeds could be the good information, or teaching imparted to the children. I knew that the time had come. In the spring of 1983 I returned home to start a school among my people.

Describing one's efforts as led by God can do several kinds of discursive work. It might presage success, or potential for success, especially if it is linked with religious narratives with a known outcome. A reader of this text who is familiar with the parable of the seeds knows that the seeds sown on "good ground" bear fruit. If God brought this text to the woman's mind, along with the vision of a school, a reader may believe that God will bless the school's efforts with "fruits." Second, describing one's efforts as a calling can justify the choice of a particular place or type of project that might otherwise seem arbitrary. After a civil war, why start a school, rather than rebuilding houses or sheltering orphans? Claiming one is "shown what to do" by God provides purchase for a particular approach to complex problems of poverty.

A volunteer from Wellsprings of Hope described her efforts to find sponsors for Ugandan children with an evangelical frame of "witness." Volunteer: So none of my . . . my family doesn't sponsor. They pray for me. They know I'm active. I . . . Interviewer: Yeah. They probably have their own passions too, right?

Volunteer: I don't know. I don't know. I feel like since I've been so frustrated that I haven't been a good enough witness or something. (Emphasis mine.)

paragraphs: Religious networks play critical roles for grassroots NGOs in recruiting supporters and offering entrée to communities in the global South. It is well established in the literature that American religious congregations are tightly networked organizations in their communities and are fertile ground for recruiting volunteers and charitable gifts (Becker and Dhingra 2001;Bekkers and Schuyt 2008;Cornwell and Harrison 2004;Musick and Wilson 2008;Patillo-McCoy 1998;Putnam and Campbell 2010). Recall that defining qualities of grassroots NGOs are private funding from individual donors and their reliance on volunteer labor. These organizations rely on personal networks of their founders to collect the goods and labor to initiate their projects. Colleagues, neighbors, family, and friends figure prominently in this support network-as do religious congregations, which often overlap with circles of family and friends. (NGO leaders Natalie and Erasto are good examples.) Religion offers networks of individuals and congregational donors that supply critical cash, labor, meeting space, and in-kind goods. And while larger aid agencies can initiate their work through partnerships with foundations, host country governments, or U.N. agencies, grassroots NGOs often gain footholds in developing countries through global religious networks (Offutt 2015;Wuthnow 2009).

Religious congregations are the most common network partner cited by grassroots NGOs on their websites. Forty-six percent of NGOs have some sort of partnership with a religious congregation. Of the 69 NGOs with a congregational partner in the sample of 150 organizations, the share that have partnerships with a U.S. congregation only, a host-country congregation only, and with both types are roughly equal. Religious congregations are more common partners than either fellow international NGOs, foreign or U.S. governments, businesses, foundations, arts groups, schools or universities, or diaspora organizations.

Websites depict religious networks as playing a critical role in furnishing donations and volunteers. Congregations can be important stops on fundraising tours; an NGO to support Tibetan Buddhism raised money through a series of visits by a well-known monk to U.S. meditation centers, yoga studios, and interfaith churches. Congregations also often provide recruitment pools for teams of volunteers that travel together for a week or two at a time. Most often these groups are referred to as "mission teams," reflecting the religious frames that are shared by the NGOs and congregations. Aid organizations that named U.S. congregations as partners on their website were significantly more likely (p < .05) to solicit American volunteers to come abroad (47 percent) than those who listed no such partnership (29 percent).

The literature on short-term missions (Probasco 2013;ver Beek 2006;Wuthnow 2009) has questioned whether short-term volunteers' contributions to aid efforts are worth the expense. After all, would it not be more efficient to contribute volunteers' travel costs toward actual project expenses? In case study interviews, American NGO leaders-and to my surprise, African leaders-said that the contributions of short-term volunteers were worth the expense and hassle. They recognize that short-term volunteers would not realistically donate the amount they spend on travel. For these volunteers, the trips abroad replace vacations or other leisure-time activities. Natalie of FKT and several board members of Wellsprings of Hope explained that short-term trips ratchet up supporters' commitment to the NGO. Trips abroad become the springboard for future financial support or membership on the board. Existing research suggests that this is a dicey proposition-ver Beek ( 2006) documented that 162 short-term missionaries to Honduras gave on average only $2 more per person, per year after their trip than before. But grassroots NGOs, operating on small budgets, do not think in terms of marginal gains among large numbers of givers; instead, they hope for major commitments from a handful, and sometimes they get them. Clara, a supporter of For Kenya's Tomorrow, came to volunteer full-time for four months in Kenya after a shorter trip. A volunteer for Activate Tanzania was convinced by villagers to raise money to build a preschool, which he did-roughly $20,000.

Several African interlocutors working with American organizations emphasized that they enjoyed the exchange with American volunteers. Paul, a Ugandan pastor who is one of the local leaders of Wellsprings of Hope, explained the utility of American volunteers in another way. "Our people, they are very, very careful and attentive to a foreigner, the volunteer, to what he is saying . . . they hear and they trust the words of the volunteers." He believed that American strangers are trusted more than Ugandan strangers also claiming to do good because Ugandans have seen too many charlatans among their own countrymen. From his perspective, why would it be worth an American's time to come halfway across the world for a swindle?

Religious networks offered financial or volunteer support to four out of the five case study NGOs. Congregational networks formed the backbone of For Kenya's Tomorrow and Wellsprings of Hope, but also were valuable to Activate Tanzania and the Rwanda Ultrasound Initiative, groups that did not invoke religious frames in their websites or in interviews and that did not carry out any evangelistic tasks. The "Introduction" section of this article explained how the global network of Lutheran churches that links Tanzania and Minnesota helped give rise to Activate Tanzania. And while several of the doctors involved with RUI spoke of the importance of separating religious practice from the provision of medical care, one of the physicians on the board had given a talk at her parents' friends' church. Religious networks were an acceptable resource for RUI even though religious modes of action and frames were not.

It was also religious networks that offered beachheads to two of the case study NGOs. Wellsprings of Hope had its beginning in a visit from a Texas delegation of Baptist preachers, and the initial goal was to provide financial support for the ministry of five Ugandan pastors. When Natalie of For Kenya's Tomorrow first traveled to that country with only a vague idea of the work she might do, a church friend sent her with an introduction to the leader of a fellow Open Bible church in Nairobi. Through social ties to that church Natalie eventually found Ruth, pastor of a small Pentecostal church. Ruth's church became FKT's main partner and her son David later became Natalie's husband. Ruth's family and close friends became the trusted network upon which Natalie built the organization's work in Nairobi, even as her personal network provided financial and administrative support in the United States.

Grassroots NGOs are typically built on personal relationships-marriages, friendships, adoptions-between Americans and individuals in the global South. Yet personal relationshipseven those bound up with religion-are perilous foundations for an aid organization if they are not reinforced by more formal structures. Bonhomie can mask inefficiency and even outright fraud, and these are difficult to detect when the American leaders of grassroots NGOs visit project sites only a few weeks per year. Bill, the American pastor that founded Wellsprings of Hope, described the five Ugandan pastors that ran the organization there as his "best friends." Yet early in the group's history one of these men embezzled thousands of dollars from the organization. The American congregation that supported the NGO was on the verge of terminating its funds, forcing Bill to fly to Uganda where the disgraced Ugandan pastor offered his confession, resignation, and a public apology. Wellsprings then established formal standards for financial accounting and increased the board's oversight.

In sum, religious networks help grassroots NGOs connect to potential volunteers and donors. Religious networks also substitute for the organizational partnerships that larger aid agencies use to establish their work in recipient countries. Grassroots NGOs need networks and amicable relationships to initiate projects, and these personal relationships are one of the things valued most by the American volunteers I interviewed. But as organizations develop, these personal relationships are best checked by formal systems of accountability.

Most often these groups are referred to as "mission teams," reflecting the religious frames that are shared by the NGOs and congregations. Aid organizations that named U.S. congregations as partners on their website were significantly more likely (p < .05) to solicit American volunteers to come abroad (47 percent) than those who listed no such partnership (29 percent).

The literature on short-term missions (Probasco 2013;ver Beek 2006;Wuthnow 2009) has questioned whether short-term volunteers' contributions to aid efforts are worth the expense. After all, would it not be more efficient to contribute volunteers' travel costs toward actual project expenses? In case study interviews, American NGO leaders-and to my surprise, African leaders-said that the contributions of short-term volunteers were worth the expense and hassle. They recognize that short-term volunteers would not realistically donate the amount they spend on travel. For these volunteers, the trips abroad replace vacations or other leisure-time activities. Natalie of FKT and several board members of Wellsprings of Hope explained that short-term trips ratchet up supporters' commitment to the NGO. Trips abroad become the springboard for future financial support or membership on the board. Existing research suggests that this is a dicey proposition-ver Beek ( 2006) documented that 162 short-term missionaries to Honduras gave on average only $2 more per person, per year after their trip than before. But grassroots NGOs, operating on small budgets, do not think in terms of marginal gains among large numbers of givers; instead, they hope for major commitments from a handful, and sometimes they get them. Clara, a supporter of For Kenya's Tomorrow, came to volunteer full-time for four months in Kenya after a shorter trip. A volunteer for Activate Tanzania was convinced by villagers to raise money to build a preschool, which he did-roughly $20,000.

Several African interlocutors working with American organizations emphasized that they enjoyed the exchange with American volunteers. Paul, a Ugandan pastor who is one of the local leaders of Wellsprings of Hope, explained the utility of American volunteers in another way. "Our people, they are very, very careful and attentive to a foreigner, the volunteer, to what he is saying . . . they hear and they trust the words of the volunteers." He believed that American strangers are trusted more than Ugandan strangers also claiming to do good because Ugandans have seen too many charlatans among their own countrymen. From his perspective, why would it be worth an American's time to come halfway across the world for a swindle?

Religious networks offered financial or volunteer support to four out of the five case study NGOs. Congregational networks formed the backbone of For Kenya's Tomorrow and Wellsprings of Hope, but also were valuable to Activate Tanzania and the Rwanda Ultrasound Initiative, groups that did not invoke religious frames in their websites or in interviews and that did not carry out any evangelistic tasks. The "Introduction" section of this article explained how the global network of Lutheran churches that links Tanzania and Minnesota helped give rise to Activate Tanzania. And while several of the doctors involved with RUI spoke of the importance of separating religious practice from the provision of medical care, one of the physicians on the board had given a talk at her parents' friends' church. Religious networks were an acceptable resource for RUI even though religious modes of action and frames were not.

It was also religious networks that offered beachheads to two of the case study NGOs. Wellsprings of Hope had its beginning in a visit from a Texas delegation of Baptist preachers, and the initial goal was to provide financial support for the ministry of five Ugandan pastors. When Natalie of For Kenya's Tomorrow first traveled to that country with only a vague idea of the work she might do, a church friend sent her with an introduction to the leader of a fellow Open Bible church in Nairobi. Through social ties to that church Natalie eventually found Ruth, pastor of a small Pentecostal church. Ruth's church became FKT's main partner and her son David later became Natalie's husband. Ruth's family and close friends became the trusted network upon which Natalie built the organization's work in Nairobi, even as her personal network provided financial and administrative support in the United States.

Grassroots NGOs are typically built on personal relationships-marriages, friendships, adoptions-between Americans and individuals in the global South. Yet personal relationshipseven those bound up with religion-are perilous foundations for an aid organization if they are not reinforced by more formal structures. Bonhomie can mask inefficiency and even outright fraud, and these are difficult to detect when the American leaders of grassroots NGOs visit project sites only a few weeks per year. Bill, the American pastor that founded Wellsprings of Hope, described the five Ugandan pastors that ran the organization there as his "best friends." Yet early in the group's history one of these men embezzled thousands of dollars from the organization. The American congregation that supported the NGO was on the verge of terminating its funds, forcing Bill to fly to Uganda where the disgraced Ugandan pastor offered his confession, resignation, and a public apology. Wellsprings then established formal standards for financial accounting and increased the board's oversight.

In sum, religious networks help grassroots NGOs connect to potential volunteers and donors. Religious networks also substitute for the organizational partnerships that larger aid agencies use to establish their work in recipient countries. Grassroots NGOs need networks and amicable relationships to initiate projects, and these personal relationships are one of the things valued most by the American volunteers I interviewed. But as organizations develop, these personal relationships are best checked by formal systems of accountability.
sections.preview_head: Religious networks play critical roles for grassroots NGOs in recruiting supporters and offering entrée to communities in the global South. It is well established in the literature that American religious congregations are tightly networked organizations in their communities and are fertile ground for recruiting volunteers and charitable gifts (Becker and Dhingra 2001;Bekkers and Schuyt 2008;Cornwell and Harrison 2004;Musick and Wilson 2008;Patillo-McCoy 1998;Putnam and Campbell 2010). Recall that defining qualities of grassroots NGOs are private funding from individual donors and their reliance on volunteer labor.

paragraphs: Having recruited supporters through religious networks, NGO leaders can draw on religious modes of action in which these individuals are already fluent. The turn towards practice in the study of religion has emphasized the habits or patterns of action that religion affords (Bender 2010;Hall 1997;Kurien 2007;Mahmood 2005, Orsi 1996). Here, the many things that people have learned to do in religious life-cook, pray, give money-are deployed for the good of aid projects. Religious modes of action taken on behalf of grassroots NGOs fall into two broad types: first, repertoires of asking for and giving time and other support; second, repertoires of religious practice that provide shared activities for Americans and locals in aid-receiving countries.

Natalie, the founder of For Kenya's Tomorrow, told me that her short-term mission trips provided a mode of action for raising FKT's early funds. As a teenager she had sent a letter to extended family and friends asking for contributions for her travel. People were willing to contribute small amounts, she told me. They did so again when Natalie started FKT. She added that her parents had been on the receiving end of many similar letters from missionaries over the years, and that they typically made small contributions; people understood that this is how mission work was paid for. Her childhood church also saw support for FKT as part of its missions repertoire: Natalie and her husband are pictured, wearing matching FKT t-shirts, in the church's directory of missionaries.

Another religious practice of giving harnessed by NGOs is the Muslim obligation of zakat, used by two groups in the website sample. These groups carried out education and sanitation projects, but also distributed food aid during Ramadan. One of these groups, based in Dayton, Ohio and called HOPE, further allows donors to designate their contributions as zakat al-maal, alms given as a "tax" of one's wealth to the poor, or zakat al-fitr, alms given on the holiday of Eid-al-fitr.

Prayer is another religious mode of action exploited by grassroots NGOs. The websites of 24 of 150 NGOs explicitly asked the reader to pray for the success of the organization. In these texts, prayer is often presented as a way for those who are unable to volunteer overseas to support the organization; in some cases, readers are encouraged to pray about how they can best contribute to the organization, making prayer a stepping stone to further commitment. Circle of Hope, which has a variety of projects in a community in Malawi, wrote that that it was starting a "24-hour prayer team . . . for the ministry and vision that Jesus has given us for the healing of Africa" (Circle of Hope 2013). The post continued with a guide for prayer adapted from a book by evangelist missionary E. Stanley Jones. Yet, consistent with the claim that religious modes of action are one of multiple repertoires used by grassroots NGOs, the next blog post on Circle of Hope's site was about an animal husbandry project; readers were asked to donate money to buy chickens and goats.

Grassroots NGOs operate in a broader context in which media and migration diffuse religious repertoires across borders (Jenkins 2002;Levitt 2001Levitt , 2007;;Miller and Yamamori 2007;Offutt 2015;Wuthnow and Offutt 2008). "Bridging" modes of action help grassroots NGOs reinforce religious networks, building trust and providing a starting point for on-the-ground collaboration between Americans and local partners. Informants from two case study NGOs told me that "prayer walks" were one of the activities that American volunteers shared with the NGOs' local leadership. A small group of Americans and locals would set out on foot in a neighborhood, calling on each house and asking if they could pray with them. Wellsprings of Hope operates in a Ugandan town that has a slight Muslim majority, and one informant told me that they made calls to both Muslim and Christian households: "We just pray together, Muslim, Christian, it doesn't really matter . . . very few people don't want to be prayed for." Clara, a 22-year-old American woman who volunteers with For Kenya's Tomorrow, told me that prayer walks and Bible studies were the main tasks on which she was able to collaborate with young Kenyan men. The young men knew the neighborhood and provided local knowledge and safety to the volunteers; the walks helped the volunteers meet neighbors and understand local conditions, which would help them carry out future work.

Among the case study NGOs, worship services were also part of the repertoire shared by Americans and African Christians. The American and Kenyan Pentecostals I met through FKT were familiar with the same songs and preachers through the global broadcasts of the Australian Pentecostal church Hillsong. Volunteers from Wellsprings of Hope and Activate Tanzania also mentioned joining African counterparts in worship. Several of these volunteers recounted the "liveliness" of these worship services that they felt was different than their American style of worship, but they said that in spite of the differences the shared worship had afforded solidarity. Their narratives were consistent with Wuthnow's claim that common worship "increase[s] the sense among widely scattered congregations that they share a common destiny and bear responsibility for one another " (2009:91). This sense of shared destiny may be more imagined than factual, but it is likely to be emotionally salient for American supporters and may increase their material commitment to NGOs' projects.

Yet NGO volunteers saw some religious modes of action as potentially damaging to their organization's goals. Two of the RUI physicians described themselves as personally religious (one Jewish, one mainline Protestant), but they drew a firm line between their own beliefs and any expression of religion in the delivery of medical care. The founder of the Rwanda Ultrasound Initiative had seen such behavior among other organizations, and disparaged it as "a bartering system"-medical care in exchange for being proselytized. RUI volunteers rejected the language of mission. Said one: "of course, there's very good things that missionaries have done. But we're not in that day and age anymore, so I think you have to be a little bit careful." This volunteer's comment points to one of the perils of the bridging religious modes of action: by emphasizing sameness, they can mask inequalities in power between Americans and aid recipients. The RUI volunteers were explicit about the power inequity between people giving and receiving medical care, and were wary that religious expression that tried to "bridge" that gap could in fact be coercive. Here is an instance where RUI volunteers' practice as secular medical professionals prevailed over religious action.

paragraphs: Religion affords particular resources to groups that operate outside of the budgets and professional paradigms of large-scale aid. First, it provides frames, or ways of thinking and speaking about relief and development work that imbue it with legitimacy. Topic models of 6,575 websites showed that 25 percent of them used the frame of Christian ministry to discuss their work, which ranged from computer training to clean water. Roughly the same share of organizations in the n = 150 content analysis used discourse of "God's will" or "showing God's love" to rationalize their work, including two groups that used this language in the context of Islam. Second, religion can offer networks for recruiting donors and volunteers, and for gaining entrée into aid-receiving communities. Finally, religion affords modes of action that link the NGO, supporters, and local aid recipients. Grassroots NGOs rely on habits of giving money and time (including time in prayer) that supporters have learned in religious practice; the groups also use religious modes of action common to the United States and aid-receiving countries to forge affective bonds and to provide tasks for American volunteers.

By focusing on a new and unstudied population of NGOs, this article fills a gap in the literature on religion and development about how the scale of aid initiatives and their relative professional or voluntary quality shapes the role of religion. As I have argued, grassroots NGOs are distinct from other development organizations in several ways. Their primary objective by IRS judgment is relief and development, and they are separate from denominational aid projects. Yet unlike larger secular organizations, they lack full-time staff who are oriented to the field of professional aid. They work with small budgets and are strongly influenced by their founders' personal relationships and ideas about development. Grassroots NGO leaders draw on "cultured capacities" (Swidler 2001) learned from work, religion, and civic life. While this article has focused on what religion affords, I have pointed to instances where religious resources are inadequate or are set aside in favor of other resources.

Like larger aid agencies, these groups need income, public legitimacy, and local partners. But grassroots NGOs meet these needs without the advantages of name recognition, professional training and staff, or connections with host country governments or U.N. agencies. Americans launch grassroots NGOs in spite of these difficulties because they value the personal, expressive opportunities that a small organization affords. Natalie's words at the beginning of the article allude to the importance of personal action: "Whether or not you are a Christian or a Muslim, or a Jew or whatever, I wanted it to be an open door for people to come and serve." Given that essentially every member of FKT's support network was a Pentecostal, I suggest that her concern here was largely symbolic; Natalie saw providing "an open door for people to come and serve" as one of FKT's crucial functions. In other words, it is not just the sanitation projects for Kenyans that matter, but the opportunity for Americans to support those projects with their own hands. The contradictory ways that these American volunteers use religion should reshape our idea of the role of religion in development aid.

By focusing on a new and unstudied population of NGOs, this article fills a gap in the literature on religion and development about how the scale of aid initiatives and their relative professional or voluntary quality shapes the role of religion. As I have argued, grassroots NGOs are distinct from other development organizations in several ways. Their primary objective by IRS judgment is relief and development, and they are separate from denominational aid projects. Yet unlike larger secular organizations, they lack full-time staff who are oriented to the field of professional aid. They work with small budgets and are strongly influenced by their founders' personal relationships and ideas about development. Grassroots NGO leaders draw on "cultured capacities" (Swidler 2001) learned from work, religion, and civic life. While this article has focused on what religion affords, I have pointed to instances where religious resources are inadequate or are set aside in favor of other resources.

Like larger aid agencies, these groups need income, public legitimacy, and local partners. But grassroots NGOs meet these needs without the advantages of name recognition, professional training and staff, or connections with host country governments or U.N. agencies. Americans launch grassroots NGOs in spite of these difficulties because they value the personal, expressive opportunities that a small organization affords. Natalie's words at the beginning of the article allude to the importance of personal action: "Whether or not you are a Christian or a Muslim, or a Jew or whatever, I wanted it to be an open door for people to come and serve." Given that essentially every member of FKT's support network was a Pentecostal, I suggest that her concern here was largely symbolic; Natalie saw providing "an open door for people to come and serve" as one of FKT's crucial functions. In other words, it is not just the sanitation projects for Kenyans that matter, but the opportunity for Americans to support those projects with their own hands. The contradictory ways that these American volunteers use religion should reshape our idea of the role of religion in development aid.
sections.preview_head: Religion affords particular resources to groups that operate outside of the budgets and professional paradigms of large-scale aid. First, it provides frames, or ways of thinking and speaking about relief and development work that imbue it with legitimacy. Topic models of 6,575 websites showed that 25 percent of them used the frame of Christian ministry to discuss their work, which ranged from computer training to clean water.
